titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to John Ross, 27 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924) to John Ross. Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. This letter deals with Montgomery's opinions on the Irish Question and a letter that he had drafted to send to the 'Times' newspaper. He also refers to Sir Edward Carson, the Ulster Unionist leader, having said the right thing 'for the moment' and the difficulties of Carson's position.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 27th May, 1916. My dear Ross, Since I saw you Asquith has joined the category of
those regarding whom my letter inquired whether they were
idiots or traitors. This makes the wisdom of publishing
my letter still more doubtful than it was. I have, however,
sent it with a paragraph on the lines of your proposed Addendum
to my son Hubert to do whatever he thinks wise with. A good
deal of the contents of my letter are embodied in the article
in yesterday's "Morning Post" and today's "Irish Times." Edward Carson said quite the right thing on Thursday
for the moment. I wonder what he will say to Llyod George.
The situation is extraordinarily difficult. Redmond is
obviously in a hole, and possibly Carson will be able to take
advantage of that to get terms tolerably satisfactory to us
without allowing himself to be manoeuvred by the radicals
and nationalists into a position in which he can be represented
as the only obstacle to a friendly settlement. Yours very truly,
